title: How John Coltrane Introduced the World to His Radical Sound with His Recording of ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things‚ÄĚ (1961)
description: John Coltrane released ‚Äúmore sig¬≠nif¬≠i¬≠cant works‚ÄĚ than his 1960 ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things,‚ÄĚ says Robin Wash¬≠ing¬≠ton in a PRX doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary on the
body:
John Coltrane released ‚Äúmore sig¬≠nif¬≠i¬≠cant works‚ÄĚ than his 1960 ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things,‚ÄĚ says Robin Wash¬≠ing¬≠ton in a PRX doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary on the clas¬≠sic rework¬≠ing of Rodgers and Hammerstein‚Äôs Broad¬≠way hit. ‚ÄúA Love Supreme‚ÄĚ is often cit¬≠ed as the zenith of the saxophonist‚Äôs career. ‚ÄúBut if you tried to explain that song to an aver¬≠age lis¬≠ten¬≠er, you would lose them. [‚ÄúMy Favorite Things‚ÄĚ] is a defin¬≠i¬≠tive work that every¬≠one knows, and any¬≠one can lis¬≠ten to, and the fas¬≠ci¬≠nat¬≠ing sto¬≠ry of its evo¬≠lu¬≠tion is some¬≠thing every¬≠one can share and enjoy.‚ÄĚ The song is acces¬≠si¬≠ble, a com¬≠mer¬≠cial¬≠ly suc¬≠cess¬≠ful hit, and it is also an exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal mas¬≠ter¬≠piece.
Indeed, ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things‚ÄĚ may be the per¬≠fect intro¬≠duc¬≠tion to Coltrane‚Äôs exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal¬≠ism. After the dizzy¬≠ing chord changes of 1959‚Äôs ‚ÄúGiant Steps,‚ÄĚ this 14-minute, two-chord excur¬≠sion pat¬≠terned on the ragas of Ravi Shankar announced Coltrane‚Äôs move into the modal forms he refined until his death in 1967, as well as his embrace of the sopra¬≠no sax¬≠o¬≠phone and his new quar¬≠tet. It became ‚ÄúColtrane‚Äôs most request¬≠ed tune,‚ÄĚ says Ed Wheel¬≠er in The World Accord¬≠ing to John Coltrane, ‚Äúand a bridge to a broad pub¬≠lic audi¬≠ence.‚ÄĚ
Coltrane‚Äôs take is also mes¬≠mer¬≠iz¬≠ing, trance-induc¬≠ing, ‚Äúoften com¬≠pared to a whirling dervish,‚ÄĚ notes the Poly¬≠phon¬≠ic video above, a ref¬≠er¬≠ence to the Sufi med¬≠i¬≠ta¬≠tion tech¬≠nique of spin¬≠ning in a cir¬≠cle. It‚Äôs an unlike¬≠ly song choice for the exer¬≠cise, which makes it all the more fas¬≠ci¬≠nat¬≠ing. The Sound of Music, Rodgers and Hammerstein‚Äôs final Broad¬≠way col¬≠lab¬≠o¬≠ra¬≠tion, was an ‚Äúinstant clas¬≠sic,‚ÄĚ and every¬≠one who‚Äôd seen it walked away hum¬≠ming the tune to ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things.‚ÄĚ By 1960, it had become a stan¬≠dard, with sev¬≠er¬≠al cov¬≠er ver¬≠sions released by Leslie Uggams, The Pete King Chorale, the Hi-Lo‚Äôs, and the Nor¬≠man Luboff Choir.


	

Hun¬≠dreds more cov¬≠ers would fol¬≠low. None of them sound¬≠ed like Coltrane‚Äôs. The modal form‚ÄĒin which musi¬≠cians impro¬≠vise in dif¬≠fer¬≠ent kinds of scales over sim¬≠pli¬≠fied chord structures‚ÄĒcreated the ‚Äúopen free¬≠dom‚ÄĚ in music explored on Miles Davis‚Äô path¬≠break¬≠ing Kind of Blue, on which Coltrane played tenor sax. (It was Davis who bought Coltrane his first sopra¬≠no sax that year.) Coltrane‚Äôs use of modal form in adap¬≠ta¬≠tions of pop¬≠u¬≠lar stan¬≠dards like ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things‚ÄĚ and George Gershwin‚Äôs ‚ÄúSum¬≠mer¬≠time‚ÄĚ from Por¬≠gy and Bess¬†was an explic¬≠it strat¬≠e¬≠gy to court a wider pub¬≠lic, using the famil¬≠iar to ori¬≠ent his lis¬≠ten¬≠ers to the new.
The video essay brings in the exper¬≠tise of musi¬≠cian, com¬≠pos¬≠er, and YouTu¬≠ber Adam Neely, who explains what makes Rogers and Hammerstein‚Äôs clas¬≠sic unique among show tunes, and why it appealed to Coltrane as the cen¬≠ter¬≠piece of the 1961 album of the same name. The song‚Äôs unusu¬≠al form and struc¬≠ture allow the same melody to be played over both major and minor chords. Coltrane‚Äôs mod¬≠i¬≠fi¬≠ca¬≠tion of the song reduces it to the two ton¬≠ics, E major and E minor, over which he and the band solo, intro¬≠duc¬≠ing a shift¬≠ing tonal¬≠i¬≠ty and mood to the melody with each chord change.


	

Neely goes into greater depth, but it‚Äôs over¬≠all an acces¬≠si¬≠ble expla¬≠na¬≠tion of Coltrane‚Äôs very acces¬≠si¬≠ble, yet ver¬≠tig¬≠i¬≠nous¬≠ly deep, ‚ÄúMy Favorite Things.‚ÄĚ Maybe only one ques¬≠tion remains. Coltrane‚Äôs ren¬≠di¬≠tion came out four years before Julie Andrews‚Äô icon¬≠ic per¬≠for¬≠mance in the film adap¬≠ta¬≠tion of The Sound of Music, evok¬≠ing the obvi¬≠ous ques¬≠tion,‚ÄĚ says Wash¬≠ing¬≠ton: ‚ÄúDid he influ¬≠ence her?‚ÄĚ
If you would like to sup¬≠port the mis¬≠sion of Open Cul¬≠ture, con¬≠sid¬≠er mak¬≠ing¬†a dona¬≠tion to our site. It‚Äôs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con¬≠tri¬≠bu¬≠tions will help us con¬≠tin¬≠ue pro¬≠vid¬≠ing the best free cul¬≠tur¬≠al and edu¬≠ca¬≠tion¬≠al mate¬≠ri¬≠als to learn¬≠ers every¬≠where. You can con¬≠tribute through Pay¬≠Pal, Patre¬≠on, and Ven¬≠mo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
Jazz Decon¬≠struct¬≠ed: What Makes John Coltrane‚Äôs ‚ÄúGiant Steps‚ÄĚ So Ground¬≠break¬≠ing and Rad¬≠i¬≠cal?
John Coltrane Draws a Pic¬≠ture Illus¬≠trat¬≠ing the Math¬≠e¬≠mat¬≠ics of Music
Dis¬≠cov¬≠er the Church of St. John Coltrane, Found¬≠ed on the Divine Music of¬†A Love Supreme
Behold John Coltrane‚Äôs Hand¬≠writ¬≠ten Out¬≠line for His Mas¬≠ter¬≠piece¬†A Love Supreme
Josh Jones is a writer and musi¬≠cian based in Durham, NC.¬†

title: When the Nobel Prize Committee Rejected The Lord of the Rings: Tolkien ‚ÄúHas Not Measured Up to Storytelling of the Highest Quality‚ÄĚ (1961)
description: When J.R.R. Tolkien‚Äôs¬†Lord of the Rings¬†books appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met with¬†very¬†mixed reviews, an unsur¬≠pris¬≠ing recep¬≠tion giv¬≠
body:
When J.R.R. Tolkien‚Äôs¬†Lord of the Rings¬†books appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met with¬†very¬†mixed reviews, an unsur¬≠pris¬≠ing recep¬≠tion giv¬≠en that noth¬≠ing like them had been writ¬≠ten for adult read¬≠ers since Edmund Spenser‚Äôs epic 16th cen¬≠tu¬≠ry Eng¬≠lish poem¬†The Faerie Queene, per¬≠haps. At least, this was the con¬≠tention of review¬≠er Richard Hugh¬≠es, who went on to write that ‚Äúfor width of imag¬≠i¬≠na¬≠tion,‚ÄĚ¬†The Lord of the Rings¬†‚Äúalmost beg¬≠gars par¬≠al¬≠lel.‚ÄĚ
Scot¬≠tish writer Nao¬≠mi Mitchi¬≠son did find a com¬≠par¬≠i¬≠son: to Sir Thomas Mal¬≠o¬≠ry, author of the 15th cen¬≠tu¬≠ry¬†Le Morte d‚ÄôArthur ‚ÄĒ hard¬≠ly mis¬≠placed, giv¬≠en Tolkien‚Äôs day job as an Oxford don of Eng¬≠lish lit¬≠er¬≠a¬≠ture, but not the sort of thing that passed for con¬≠tem¬≠po¬≠rary writ¬≠ing in the 1950s, notwith¬≠stand¬≠ing the seri¬≠ous appre¬≠ci¬≠a¬≠tion of writ¬≠ers like W.H. Auden for Tolkien‚Äôs tril¬≠o¬≠gy. ‚ÄúNo pre¬≠vi¬≠ous writer,‚ÄĚ the poet remarked in a¬†New York Times review, ‚Äúhas, to my knowl¬≠edge, cre¬≠at¬≠ed an imag¬≠i¬≠nary world and a feigned his¬≠to¬≠ry in such detail.‚ÄĚ
Auden did find fault with Tolkien‚Äôs poet¬≠ry, a fact upon which crit¬≠ic Edmund Wil¬≠son seized in his scathing 1956 Lord of the Rings¬†review. ‚ÄúMr. Auden is appar¬≠ent¬≠ly quite insen¬≠si¬≠tive ‚ÄĒ through lack of inter¬≠est in the oth¬≠er depart¬≠ment,‚ÄĚ wrote Wil¬≠son, ‚Äúto the fact that Tolkien‚Äôs prose is just as bad. Prose and verse are on the same lev¬≠el of pro¬≠fes¬≠so¬≠r¬≠i¬≠al ama¬≠teur¬≠ish¬≠ness.‚ÄĚ Five years lat¬≠er, the Nobel prize jury would make the same judge¬≠ment when they exclud¬≠ed Tolkien‚Äôs books from con¬≠sid¬≠er¬≠a¬≠tion. Tolkien‚Äôs prose, wrote jury mem¬≠ber Anders √Ėster¬≠ling, ‚Äúhas not in any way mea¬≠sured up to sto¬≠ry¬≠telling of the high¬≠est qual¬≠i¬≠ty.‚ÄĚ
The note was dis¬≠cov¬≠ered recent¬≠ly by Swedish jour¬≠nal¬≠ist Andreas Ekstr√∂m, who delved into the Nobel archive for 1961 and found that ‚Äúthe jury passed over names includ¬≠ing Lawrence Dur¬≠rell, Robert Frost, Gra¬≠ham Greene, E.M. Forster, and Tolkien to come up with their even¬≠tu¬≠al win¬≠ner, Yugosla¬≠vian writer Ivo Andrińá,‚ÄĚ as Ali¬≠son Flood reports at¬†The Guardian. (The Nobel archives are sealed until 50 years after the year the award is giv¬≠en.) Ekstr√∂m has been read¬≠ing through the archives ‚Äúfor the past five years or so,‚ÄĚ he says, ‚Äúand this was the first time I have seen Tolkien‚Äôs name among the sug¬≠gest¬≠ed can¬≠di¬≠dates.‚ÄĚ His name appeared on the list chiefly through the machi¬≠na¬≠tions of his clos¬≠est friend and chief sup¬≠port¬≠er, C.S. Lewis.
Lewis, ‚Äúalso of Oxford,‚ÄĚ Wil¬≠son sneered, ‚Äúis able to top them all‚ÄĚ in praise of Tolkien‚Äôs books. From the first appear¬≠ance of his Mid¬≠dle Earth fan¬≠ta¬≠sy in¬†The Hob¬≠bit,¬†Lewis promised to ‚Äúdo all in my pow¬≠er to secure for Tolkien‚Äôs great book the recog¬≠ni¬≠tion it deserves,‚ÄĚ as he wrote in a 1953 let¬≠ter to British pub¬≠lish¬≠er Stan¬≠ley Unwin. In what might be con¬≠sid¬≠ered an uneth¬≠i¬≠cal pro¬≠mo¬≠tion of his friend‚Äôs work today, Lewis respond¬≠ed tire¬≠less¬≠ly to crit¬≠ics of the tril¬≠o¬≠gy, going so far, after the pub¬≠li¬≠ca¬≠tion of¬†The Two Tow¬≠ers, to pen an essay on the sub¬≠ject titled ‚ÄúThe Dethrone¬≠ment of Pow¬≠er.‚ÄĚ Here, Lewis explains the pro¬≠lix qual¬≠i¬≠ty of Tolkien‚Äôs prose ‚ÄĒ that which crit¬≠ics called ‚Äútedious‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ as a nar¬≠ra¬≠tive neces¬≠si¬≠ty: ‚ÄúI do not think he could have done it any oth¬≠er way.‚ÄĚ
Tolkien‚Äôs biggest fan also urged read¬≠ers to spend more time with the books and promised that the rewards would be great. In defense of the sec¬≠ond work of the tril¬≠o¬≠gy, he con¬≠clud¬≠ed, ‚Äúthe book is too orig¬≠i¬≠nal and too opu¬≠lent for any final judg¬≠ment on a first read¬≠ing. But we know at once that it has done things to us. We are not quite the same men. And though we must ration our¬≠selves in our reread¬≠ings, I have lit¬≠tle doubt that the book will soon take its place among the indis¬≠pens¬≠ables.‚ÄĚ And so has all of Tolkien‚Äôs work, becom¬≠ing the lit¬≠er¬≠ary stan¬≠dard by which high fan¬≠ta¬≠sy is mea¬≠sured, with or with¬≠out a Nobel prize.
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2021.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
J.R.R. Tolkien Snubs a Ger¬≠man Pub¬≠lish¬≠er Ask¬≠ing for Proof of His ‚ÄúAryan Descent‚ÄĚ (1938)
110 Draw¬≠ings and Paint¬≠ings by J.R.R. Tolkien: Of Mid¬≠dle-Earth and Beyond
J.R.R. Tolkien Expressed a ‚ÄúHeart¬≠felt Loathing‚ÄĚ for Walt Dis¬≠ney and Refused to Let Dis¬≠ney Stu¬≠dios Adapt His Work
Dis¬≠cov¬≠er J.R.R. Tolkien‚Äôs Lit¬≠tle-Known and Hand-Illus¬≠trat¬≠ed Children‚Äôs Book, Mr. Bliss
When J.R.R. Tolkien Worked for the¬†Oxford Eng¬≠lish Dic¬≠tio¬≠nary¬†and ‚ÄúLearned More ‚Ä¶ Than Any Oth¬≠er Equal Peri¬≠od of My Life‚ÄĚ (1919‚Äď1920)
Josh Jones is a writer and musi¬≠cian based in Durham, NC.¬†

body:
in Music, Television |   	May 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

It took near¬≠ly 50 years. WKRP in Cincin¬≠nati is no longer just a TV sit¬≠com. It‚Äôs now a real radio sta¬≠tion in Cincin¬≠nati.
A Cin¬≠cy-area FM sta¬≠tion, known as ‚ÄúThe Oasis,‚ÄĚ has adopt¬≠ed the WKRP call let¬≠ters after acquir¬≠ing them from a non¬≠prof¬≠it radio sta¬≠tion in North Car¬≠oli¬≠na. The Raleigh-based sta¬≠tion put the call let¬≠ters up for auc¬≠tion as part of a fundrais¬≠ing effort. And then The Oasis snapped them up.
To mark the offi¬≠cial launch last week, the sta¬≠tion played the TV show‚Äôs theme song for six straight hours. Mov¬≠ing for¬≠ward, the sta¬≠tion will con¬≠tin¬≠ue play¬≠ing clas¬≠sic rock from the ‚Äô60s through the ‚Äô80s ‚ÄĒ much like the music fea¬≠tured on the 1978‚Äď82 sit¬≠com. As a bonus, Gary Sandy, who played pro¬≠gram direc¬≠tor Andy Travis, has record¬≠ed pro¬≠mos for the revived WKRP. If the orig¬≠i¬≠nal show was before your time, you can watch some episodes on YouTube. Enjoy‚Ä¶
If you would like to sup¬≠port the mis¬≠sion of Open Cul¬≠ture, con¬≠sid¬≠er mak¬≠ing¬†a dona¬≠tion to our site. It‚Äôs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con¬≠tri¬≠bu¬≠tions will help us con¬≠tin¬≠ue pro¬≠vid¬≠ing the best free cul¬≠tur¬≠al and edu¬≠ca¬≠tion¬≠al mate¬≠ri¬≠als to learn¬≠ers every¬≠where. You can con¬≠tribute through Pay¬≠Pal, Patre¬≠on, and Ven¬≠mo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: Why The Founding Fathers Were Obsessed with This Muslim Ruler
description:
in History |   	May 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The writ¬≠ings of the Found¬≠ing Fathers of the Unit¬≠ed States of Amer¬≠i¬≠ca include many a r
body:
in History |   	May 11th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The writ¬≠ings of the Found¬≠ing Fathers of the Unit¬≠ed States of Amer¬≠i¬≠ca include many a ref¬≠er¬≠ence to the likes of Cicero, Mon¬≠tesquieu, and John Locke. That the names Hyder Ali and his son Tipu Sul¬≠tan nev¬≠er appear may not sound like much of a sur¬≠prise, even if you hap¬≠pen to know that they ruled the Indi¬≠an region of Mysore, now offi¬≠cial¬≠ly called Mysu¬≠ru, at the time. But his¬≠to¬≠ry records that more than a few Amer¬≠i¬≠cans, includ¬≠ing Thomas Jef¬≠fer¬≠son and John Adams, fol¬≠lowed with great inter¬≠est the strug¬≠gles of that father and son against the British. Those strug¬≠gles took place from the mid-eigh¬≠teenth to the ear¬≠ly nine¬≠teenth cen¬≠tu¬≠ry ‚ÄĒ a time when the Amer¬≠i¬≠can colonies, of course, had their own con¬≠flict brew¬≠ing with the moth¬≠er¬≠land.
Hyder became the Sul¬≠tan of Mysore in the sev¬≠en¬≠teen-six¬≠ties: ‚Äúa dan¬≠ger¬≠ous time to come to pow¬≠er in South Asia,‚ÄĚ writes¬†Blake Smith at Aeon, giv¬≠en that ‚Äúthe British East India Com¬≠pa¬≠ny was expand¬≠ing its pow¬≠er through¬≠out the Sub¬≠con¬≠ti¬≠nent.‚ÄĚ Ally¬≠ing with France, much like the rebelling Amer¬≠i¬≠can colonists, Hyder ‚Äúheld off the British advance for anoth¬≠er two decades, dying in 1782, just a year before the US tri¬≠umphed in its own rebel¬≠lion against Britain.‚ÄĚ
Amer¬≠i¬≠ca‚Äôs fas¬≠ci¬≠na¬≠tion with Hyder and his suc¬≠ces¬≠sor Tipu, who died in bat¬≠tle with the East India Com¬≠pa¬≠ny in 1799, remained for some time. ‚ÄúMysore‚Äôs rulers became famil¬≠iar ref¬≠er¬≠ences in Amer¬≠i¬≠can news¬≠pa¬≠pers, poems and every¬≠day con¬≠ver¬≠sa¬≠tion. Yet, with¬≠in a gen¬≠er¬≠a¬≠tion, Amer¬≠i¬≠cans lost their sense of sol¬≠i¬≠dar¬≠i¬≠ty with the Indi¬≠an Sub¬≠con¬≠ti¬≠nent.‚ÄĚ
You can learn more about this episode of his¬≠to¬≠ry from the PBS Ori¬≠gins video above. It gets into detail about the life of Tipu, known as ‚Äúthe Tiger of Mysore,‚ÄĚ a nick¬≠name the man him¬≠self did much to jus¬≠ti¬≠fy. He even ‚Äúcom¬≠mis¬≠sioned a near¬≠ly life-sized automa¬≠ton of a tiger eat¬≠ing a British sol¬≠dier,‚ÄĚ says the video‚Äôs host, which ‚Äúinclud¬≠ed a crank attached to a mech¬≠a¬≠nism inside the tiger‚Äôs body that simul¬≠ta¬≠ne¬≠ous¬≠ly lift¬≠ed the dying man‚Äôs arm and pro¬≠duced nois¬≠es imi¬≠tat¬≠ing his final cries.‚ÄĚ Though he and his army con¬≠tin¬≠ued to fight in that spir¬≠it, Mysore‚Äôs sit¬≠u¬≠a¬≠tion became unten¬≠able after both the U.S. and France made their peace with Britain. Despite the recen¬≠cy of the hos¬≠til¬≠i¬≠ties, the new lib¬≠er¬≠at¬≠ed colony soon became some¬≠thing of an ally in the main¬≠te¬≠nance of the British Empire‚Äôs remain¬≠ing ter¬≠ri¬≠to¬≠ries, India includ¬≠ed ‚ÄĒ and would ulti¬≠mate¬≠ly learn a les¬≠son or two of its own about the glob¬≠al exten¬≠sion of pow¬≠er.

India on Film, 1899‚Äď1947: An Archive of 90 His¬≠toric Films Now Online]

Based in Seoul,¬†Col¬≠in¬†Marshall¬†writes and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬†Books on Cities¬†as well as the books¬†ŪēúÍĶ≠ žöĒžēĹ ÍłąžßÄ¬†(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬†Kore¬≠an Newtro.¬†Fol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬†@colinmarshall.

title: Bespoke Glass Studio's Sculptures Challenge Traditional Conventions of Stained Glass
description: With a stained glass window, light filters through to illuminate narrative scenes or geometric patterns, but it‚Äôs primarily the window itself that
body:
With a stained glass window, light filters through to illuminate narrative scenes or geometric patterns, but it‚Äôs primarily the window itself that draws our attention. For Lesley Green of Bespoke Glass, these vibrant compositions certainly aren‚Äôt limited to these traditional apertures. ‚ÄúOne of my personal obsessions is trying to convince people to hang glass on the wall instead of in the window, so you can really experience the pure color and texture of the glass,‚ÄĚ she tells Colossal.
Bespoke Glass creates a wide range of aesthetic and functional forms, conceived for both residential and commercial interiors. Some are designed to be screens or separators, such as behind a bar or between tables in a restaurant. Others are more sculptural, such as her three-dimensional sculptures that project onto the wall when the sun shines through them, interacting with local shadows. This display method also highlights the inherent textures of the glass itself, from waves to ridges to mottled patterns.

Using a hand-cut, traditional copper foil method of creating the stained glass, Green has also innovated some studio-developed techniques to produce three-dimensional objects. ‚ÄúCraftsmanship is extremely important to me as well,‚ÄĚ she says. ‚ÄúPrecise cuts and especially smooth solder lines are part of everything my studio produces.‚ÄĚ
All pieces are available to be commissioned in custom colorways, and Green is also working on larger-scale versions of the sculptural works, plus multiple-piece collections designed to installed on the wall. See more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Bespoke Glass Studio's Sculptures Challenge Traditional Conventions of Stained Glass

title: Creative Thought Is Essential: A Letter from Our Editor
description:
Dear readers,
In a collection of her published diary entries, Virgina Woolf wrote, ‚ÄúThinking is my fighting.‚ÄĚ This sentiment was a prominent theme
body:
Dear readers,
In a collection of her published diary entries, Virgina Woolf wrote, ‚ÄúThinking is my fighting.‚ÄĚ This sentiment was a prominent theme in her 1940 essay, Thoughts on Peace In an Air Raid, in which the British writer framed the importance of individual thought as a crucial antidote to war and the rise of fascism.
I‚Äôve been thinking a lot about Woolf‚Äôs quote and its relevance today, largely connected to discourse on education. While literacy rates in the U.S. continue to plummet, a soaring number of individuals are adopting habits antithetical to thinking‚ÄĒoffloading even the smallest of cognitive tasks onto Chat GPT, doomscrolling for hours on social media, and numbing the mind with addictive ‚Äúbrain rot.‚ÄĚ Critical thinking is being traded for passive acceptance and the death of curiosity‚ÄĒall in the name of efficiency and capital innovation. Our minds have become the new commodity.
In response to this, I‚Äôve seen a clear cultural shift toward self-education. I, too, have been influenced by this new wave of autodidactic learning‚ÄĒpersonal curricula, digital detoxes, reading challenges, etc. While these are certainly more examples of internet trends worming their way into our algorithms, beneath them lies something deeper: a collective desire to reclaim our own thoughts.
At the same time, access to quality information and educational materials can be hard to find, whether those resources are locked behind the gates of inflated tuition costs, class fees, or online paywalls. For Colossal, accessibility has always been an ongoing commitment. Over the last 15 years and counting, every article, interview, and instructional aid on our site has remained completely free. Our team believes that the ability to learn about and enjoy art must be universally available.
‚ÄúI‚Äôve been using Colossal as my go-to source of art on the internet since 2012 when I was in high school. It‚Äôs been a pleasure watching it grow and keep up with the breadth and depth of the work being done in the art world.‚ÄĚJake F.
For years, members of this community have made us aware of how beneficial Colossal has been in their learning journeys. Educators of all ages have shared that they‚Äôve introduced the website to their students, crafting complete lesson plans from our articles and glossary of art terms. I encountered Colossal for the first time in a classroom, and in another life where I taught elementary students art, it remained one of my most reliable tools.
Out of the classroom, learning persists, and we‚Äôve been elated to hear many stories about how our daily doses of art allow readers to uncover something new everyday, gain more knowledge about contemporary art, and discover impactful stories that bring about change, connection, and hope.
Now more than ever, creative thought must be nurtured, protected, and free from barriers. Our mission to do this can only continue with you. Today, only 1% of Colossal readers financially support the work we do. Like many independent publications, our members are the cornerstone of our existence and ability to continue sharing art with the world.
We encourage you to join the Colossal community. Now is the time to spread creative ideas, revive curiosity, and defend the ability to think and learn imaginatively.
And as we celebrate teacher appreciation this month, we‚Äôd like to highlight our deeply discounted education membership, available to current students and educators (just shoot as a quick email and we‚Äôll get you set up). Thank you for reading, sharing, and learning with us.
Gratefully,Jackie AndresOnline editor, Colossal

schema.headline: Creative Thought Is Essential: A Letter from Our Editor

title: Habib Hajallie's Meticulous Ballpoint Pen Drawings Examine the Depths of Emotion
description: In ballpoint pen on found fragments of philosophical and historical texts, Habib Hajallie delves into the emotional realm of memory, connection, a
body:
In ballpoint pen on found fragments of philosophical and historical texts, Habib Hajallie delves into the emotional realm of memory, connection, and loss. The Kent-based artist often celebrates Black cultural figures and beloved family members, along with examining his own personal experiences as a British man of Sierra Leonean and Lebanese heritage. In his current solo exhibition, Black & Blue at Larkin Durey, Hajallie grapples with the devastating stillbirth of his daughter and the ‚Äúindescribable emotions that sit beneath language,‚ÄĚ says the gallery.
For this show, the artist deliberately switched from using black ballpoint ink to blue. As he made these works, Hajallie also reflected on the loss of his sister four years ago. Using antique maps and snippets of philosophical and sociological writings, he portrays subtle sides of what the gallery describes as ‚Äúan altered sense of self.‚ÄĚ Figures, including several self-portraits, exude feelings of despair, confusion, numbness, care, and the nuanced emotions that emerge in-between.
‚ÄúStill Remain‚ÄĚ (2026), ballpoint pen on antique texts, 11 3/8 x 16 1/2 inches
‚ÄúWhile this series is concerned with the internal landscape of loss and what it means to endure a profoundly altered reality, each artwork has acted as an invaluable step towards healing,‚ÄĚ the gallery says. ‚ÄúBy drawing directly onto antique texts that explore morality, purpose, and transcendence, Hajallie‚Äôs personal pain enters into a wider conversation about finding meaning and the ways in which drawing can become a space of solace and catharsis.‚ÄĚ
Black & Blue continues through May 22 in London. See more on the artist‚Äôs Instagram.
‚ÄúA Refuge Among Reflections‚ÄĚ (2026), ballpoint pen on antique texts, 33 1/8 x 23 1/4 inches
Detail of ‚ÄúA Refuge Among Reflections‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúThis Mind Hath Demolition Reached‚ÄĚ (2025), ballpoint pen on antique texts, 11 3/4 x 16 1/2 inches
‚ÄúNothing Else to Fear‚ÄĚ (2026), ballpoint pen on antique texts, 33 1/8 x 23 1/4 inches
‚ÄúArise and Walk Strongly and Fearlessly‚ÄĚ (2026), ballpoint pen on antique texts, 33 1/8 x 23 1/4 inches

schema.headline: Habib Hajallie's Meticulous Ballpoint Pen Drawings Examine the Depths of Emotion
